You must read and internalize these definitions before answering the questions. Please pay attention to the four schools of thought as defined, which may help in distinguishing between types. ***DOCUMENT 1: THE CODING TASK*** Section 1: # The Coding Task ***This codebook develops a systematic and standardized way to classify qualitative documents into one of Cashore’s four problem types(4PT) framework*** . It will do so by providing guidance to coders on how to assign Type 1, 2, 3 or 4 status to qualitative text including, but not limited to, peer reviewed journal articles, course syllabi, policy statements, policy analyses, media communications and policy recommendations. This classification effort complements, rather than replaces, careful qualitative application of the framework required for uncovering important nuances through deliberative analysis and reflection of highly complex text. ***What the quantitative coding effort does well is to generate useful knowledge about whether the overall patterns identified by an one study or set of studies compare to other cases outside of the study. Coding also permits assessments as to whether there are differences in the dominance of one or more problem types across time and also space – be they countries, organizations, or sectors.*** Hence, not only can a well-developed code book generate descriptions about a range of policy and governance outcomes, it will also allow for broadening epistemological and methodological techniques that might explain variation – such as the correlation between the kind of disciplinary, sub-disciplinary and interdisciplinary training experts received that shape their bias in emphasizing one Type over the other. This in turn, might help generate knowledge about, and reverse when desired, “unconscious bias” about the way policy scholars and practitioners conceive of, and measure, “on the ground” problems. In other words, this framework can help advance “fit for purpose” policy analysis. To be clear: unlike Cashore’s broader argument about the drift away from Type 4 problems that he has, and is, applying to understand their role in shaping sustainability policy and governance over the last 35 years, the code book is designed to describe a range of outcomes, to which Cashore and his collaborators, and anyone else for that matter, can better assess, test, and develop their own arguments and theories. In short, the code book, paves the way for, but does not take a position on, which of the four problem types is preferred nor the causes of changes in emphasis of the Types. Importantly, although the 4PT distinctions originally came out of Cashore’s work on global, transnational and local approaches to sustainability policy and governance, they can be applied to disentangle how any kind of problem facing society and policy making are analysed, and treated, by scholars and experts. This means that ***while Cashore applies and draws examples from sustainability policy scholarship and practice, the framework is equally useful for anyone working on problem focused policy analysis including, but not limited to those scholars working in schools of public health, education, social work, forestry, fisheries, natural resources, agriculture, and even mining and energy for that matter.*** Having said this, the guidance and examples below for coders to be able to render concrete important but sometimes abstract distinctions, draw on examples from five years of efforts to code sustainability policy and governance analyses. This code book proceeds in the following steps. First, we discuss Cashore’s “utility” and “problem solving” labels as the two key distinctions that position the 4PT framework. Second, we review Cashore’s four key schools of sustainability thought and practice – including notable strands within them – to show how each of these 4PTs are subtly nurtured by sophisticated scholarly communities. Third, we review the challenges facing coding of different kinds of qualitative documents. Fourth, we review different sorting challenges based on the kinds of document being including abstracts of course syllabi, journal articles. Fifth we offer solutions to each including reviewing of our protocol for sorting scholarly articles through careful qualitative analysis that has been developed and revised over the last five years. We then review our approach to coding course syllabi and the development of key words to facilitate swift coding of a range of documents. We then discuss our systematic efforts to advance AI assisted coding with the goal of producing systematic text analysis that would be consistent with the laborious human coding efforts of the last six years (i.e. we seek to achieve intercoder reliability between AI and humans .8 or better). We conclude by offering recommendations on next steps for both Cashore’s team and collaborators, as well as those who might want to apply the 4PT framework beyond sustainability policy and governance considerations. Appendices A reviews our approach to coding articles beyond the two distinctions as a way to generate propositions about preliminary explanations for results and Appendix B which provides examples from cases with which to assist in sorting initially “ambiguous” cases. Section 2: ## a) Key Distinctions: Utility and Problem Solving Orientations The specific coding task appears at first glance seems quite straightforward i.e. to distinguish whether scholarly and practitioner policy analyses emphasise, or not, “utility” and “problem solving” orientations (Table 1). | Table1: The Four Problem Types (4PT)
Simplified Distinctions | Utility Dominates? YES | Utility Dominates? No | | ----------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------- | --------------------- | | **Problem Solving Dominates? YES** | TYPE 1 | TYPE 4 | | **Problem Solving Dominates? NO** | TYPE 2 | TYPE 3 | These two distinctions originally emerged from Cashore’s reflections, individually and with colleagues, about why so many sustainability policy experts have disagreed for over 35 years about how to think about, and also manage, critical environmental challenges facing the planet highlighted by climate, biodiversity and forest degradation. (Again, while derived from the sustainability policy and governance cases, the 4PT distinctions are meant to be applied to any “on the ground” problems facing policy makers and society including health care, education, child care, education and so on). The origins of the 4PT distinctions followed Cashore’s recognition, having only worked in interdisciplinary forestry, environment and policy schools, that longstanding largely unconscious biases among himself, his colleagues, and experts generally, about how to study the world of sustainability policy and governance, helped explain longstanding debates about not only about how to conceive of, and measure, sustainability challenges, but also recommended courses of policy action for managing the environment, including climate, species extinctions, biodiversity loss, as well as forests and natural resources management. What Cashore noticed was two key divides within these debates. ***The first distinction*** was whether sustainability scholars and practitioners believed that enhancing **“utility”** outcomes was the key task facing sustainability policy analysis. He noted that a large group of scholars generally felt that the more utility was enhanced, the better off society will be. While this causal belief is widely diffused among a range of analysts, it is also hotly debated by scholars and analysts who either oppose utility as a guiding orientation, and/or seek other ways to frame desired outcomes. Hence, Cashore recognized that the belief that utility enhancement was the preferable, from those that did not, was an excellent way to distinguish sustainability policy and governance analyses. ***Cashore’s second distinction*** came from his recognition that while some narrowed their conclusion to assessing how to solve or effectively manage the clearly specified problem at hand- such as such as saving a particular species from extinction, avoiding resource collapse of a specified fish stock, or limiting warming to 1.5 degrees above pre-industrial levels – others brought in analytical frames that allowed for a deliberations over not only how, but ***whether*** the problem at hand could or should be solved. That is, this latter group applied adjudicating frameworks that were developed as a way to address ***any*** particular problem – regardless as to its unique features - that might find its way to the societal or policy agenda in mind. Rather than generating research and analysis to shed light on how to manage a specific policy challenge, this group was united by developing frameworks that allow for consideration of not acting at all, or acting in a way that does not effectively manage the problem at hand. Section 3: ## b) Conceptual Foundations: Utility and Problem Contingent Labels While these distinctions showed great promise, the next task was to elaborate their meanings so that their influence could be better identified and assessed. Section 4: ### The utility label covers a broad range Cashore’s 4PT framework notes that the term “utility” is generally applied and defined broadly. It includes for example, those trained in welfare economics who, largely focusing on income or economic growth, convert others outcomes to “utility equivalents” in order to monetize, and compare, human preferences for green space and nature, with their direct interest in generating income. The term “utility” also incorporates the broader philosophy of “utilitarianism” that seeks to advance outcomes that produce the “greatest good for the greatest number in the long run”. It is also used more generally as a term to discuss whether, in terms of enhancing individual or collection satisfactions, there is any “utility” in a proposed course of action? For Cashore, the rationale for use of “utility” as a label was that it best captures the general belief behind a range of analyses that their goal is to improve “**human satisfactions”.** This is an important clarification because the utility as a term has a decidedly anthropogenetic orientation in which policy is geared towards improving the interests of humans either today or in the future. This, in turn, is shaped by some kind of homage and widespread work developing theories relevant to unpacking human **“self-interest”** .What is important for coding on the 4PT framework is that the concern itself is what is important – i.e. **the utility label in the 4PT framework is shorthand to capture those who are motivated to advance human self-interested satisfactions that incorporate a range of more specific satisfaction seeking rationales** This orientation in turn, allows coders to identify efforts to promote and understand **self-interested behaviours at multiple levels** of sustainability policy and governance including individual, organizational, nation state decisions as well as those within international governance arenas and global governance agreements. Hence, the utility label includes, for coding purposes, analyses focused on aggregating **individual utility** , **organizational utility** (such as companies returning shareholder value), or even **states’ utility** by maximizing their own self-interested preferences. This category incorporates, rather than distinguishes, longstanding debates among neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars within international relations about whether self-interested, utility chasing states, are motivated to achieve relative or absolute gains. (The 4PT framework notes that debates about relative or absolute gains have undermine the similar quest to advance utility seeking interests, including, at the state level, relying on some conception of “national interest” that improves the satisfaction of its population through some kind of beneficial “rational” outcome. **Hence, this broad use of the utility label focuses coders on identifying any kind of analysis that is motivated by enhancing human satisfaction through self-interested preference maximization** . This allows coders to capture in one category a range of scholarship and analysis that although diverse and nuanced, are united by their underlying orientation in emphasizing individual, organizational and state based preferences that pay homage to improving human “satisfactions” at multiple levels. Section 5: ### The problem solving label: distinguishing analyses that target “how”, not “whether” --- The term “problem solving” is also used broadly to capture a range of expertise and approaches that undertake research with which to solve or effectively manage a particular “on the ground” problem. That is, they eschew asking “whether” a problem can be effectively managed, and instead ask, given the problem at hand, how can it be effectively managed? This category then incorporates a broad group of scholars and analysts who are united in their quest to understand and effectively management an “on the ground” problem such as the extinction of a particular species or limiting warming to no more than 1.5/2 °C above pre-industrial levels promoted through the Paris Accord. This means that the analyses they bring, and conclusions they make, will go back to implications for generating knowledge useful for problem solving of a problem, or a class of problems. Similarly, these analysts will never apply universal pre-established theories to assess whether the problem at hand ***ought*** to be solved. This is not their interest. Likewise while their research may carry lessons for other problems, they do not seek to generate universal theories that can be applied to any kind of problem. Put another way, this group of scholars would, for these reasons, avoid any quest for developing a theoretical orientation, or set of recommendations, that went beyond the problem or class of problems from which their research was derived. The practical result is that many in this category tend to carefully assess problem structure or “key features of the problem” they are attempting to shed light on how to better manage or solve. This is important. The reason they eschew any effort to develop a universal way to address any problem is because, by disconnecting from problem structure, the approach applied elsewhere might not effectively manage or solve another class of problems – i.e. it might even make those worse off. This means that they are largely conducting research in which its lessons are “problem contingent”. Any effort to generate lessons beyond a class of problems that is offered as a way to think about, and manage, all problems, is evidence that a scholarly analysis is not in this category. More specifically, any effort that concludes it is not “feasible” or “rational” to address a particular problem at hand that draws on a pre-established theory or framework that that was developed to analyse any and all problem is outside of this sorting category. This elaboration leads to the identification of a more nuanced table of which human and AI coders must be aware. Coders will note that I have also changed the Y axis label to “problem contingent” which I treat as synonymous as “problem solving” dominates, but also more useful for coding purposes. I keep the broader term in the first table as it is more accessible to the uninitiated coder, while bringing in the more specific term once they have read these first few overview pages. | Table 2: The Four Problem Types
Expanded Distinctions | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? YES | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? NO | | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? YES** | TYPE 1 Utility / Contingent | TYPE 4 Not Utility / Contingent | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? NO** | TYPE 2 Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | TYPE 3 Not Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | In sum, at the risk of repetition, let me be clear: these frameworks offer ways to deliberate over ***not only how, but whether, a specific problem can be effectively managed given a broader set of considerations beyond the problem*** itself. Section 6: ## C) Examples of Not Problem Contingent (Universal Approaches) As I discuss below I refer to the major strand of Optimization school of sustainability thought and practice known as “cost-benefit analysis” (CBA) as a leading example of this approach. This is because CBA frameworks assesses whether or not a policy intervention governing a particular problem will be able to produce, or not, aggregate utility benefits. Under this approach, if policy designers cannot find an intervention to address a problem at hand that will enhance overall utility (i.e. possible policy interventions are all deemed to undermine aggregate utility) then the “rational” answer from this CBA approach is not to act. There are, to be sure, many other strands of the Optimization school. For example, within international relations, both neo-realist and neo-liberal international relations scholars seek to generate theories of state preference, including longstanding debates about whether states maximize their preferences owing to absolute or relative gains. What is important for the 4PT framework is not their debates about underlying motivations that shape a given state’s “self-interest” preferences, but that both neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars develop their theories and frameworks to explain ***state behaviour in general*** – i.e. they seek universal understandings of utility chasing state preference that explain, but are not derived from, their choices about how to manage a specified problem. That is, they develop and apply theories of state preferences that apply beyond a particular problem, but are meant to explain state behaviour over a particular problem. To repeat, their project is to develop universal theories of state action that are not derived from, or limited to, any specific problem or issue states happen to be deliberating over. These theories of state action also inform recommendations from these scholar about how states might act go address a specific problem in order to better achieve their pre-existing self-interested preferences (i.e. how to “optimize” outcomes given existing understandings of state preferences). This is why, as I discuss below, so many IR scholars make the case for addressing the global climate crisis based on the argument that doing so will advance state preferences, rather than asking whether a theory of state preferences is the most useful way to think about, and manage, particular kinds of problems. This same dilemma facing cost-benefit scholars who so believe in the utility enhancing motivations that they constantly argue, and work so hard to demonstrate – even despite overwhelming evidence to the contrary – that an emphasis on the utility undermining effects of the climate crisis is the most useful way to conceive of, and develop policy options for, averting global warming. As I discuss below, strands within the “Compromise School” of sustainability thought and practice are also replete with a range of efforts to develop universal theories of stakeholder engagement, legitimacy formation, and conflict resolution that, while rejecting utility chasing theories, nonetheless also develop frameworks for understanding motivations of individuals, organizations and states, that are also developed to understand, and prescribe solutions for, a range of problems that may come across the desk of policy officials. For example, there is one strand of the Compromise school known as “alternative dispute resolution” ADR that offers a way for stakeholder to agree on litigation avoiding natural resources management agreements that draws on universal theories of individual and organization behaviour as a way foster a compromise agreement among disparate interests. While drawing on theories that extend far beyond, and reject, utility motivations as far to narrow an approach to human behaviour, they share the same mission in developing university theories as a way to generate an understanding about not only how, but whether, the “on the ground” issue, or set of issues, can be resolved given the overall focus on conflict resolution rather than solving and effectively managing, the “on the ground” problem that spark the ADR in the first place. Another stand of the compromise school has been nurtured by those studying philosophy which seeks to generate a set of norms and protocols for making difficult decisions – such as whether to save someone stopped on the train tracks that might risk the lives of many on the train. In these cases, pre-existing approaches for developing normative theory based protocols for doing so – not based on utility per se but on claims to higher level “ethics” are turned to as a way to have society and policy makers thinks about managing difficult questions – from species extinctions that results from enhancing economic growth – to saving lives during a pandemic when the isolationist policies for doing say may undermine children’s cognitive development. In these cases what is important for the 4PT framework is that the adjudicating analysis that result are meant to help policy makers and society make decisions to address any problem that might come along, and are not derived, narrowed to, or contingent, on a particular class of problems. Section 7: ## d) Examples of Problem Contingent Approaches (Not Universal) In contrast, and as I elaborate below, members of Commons and Prioritization schools narrowing that adjudicating frameworks to only address, and are indeed derived from, a particular “on the ground” problem or class of problems – whether it be for utility enhancing (Commons) or non-utility (Prioritization) motivations. There are many strands within each of these schools that reinforce “problem contingent” rather than non-problem contingent universal (i.e.. meant to be applied to any problems) frameworks. As I discuss below the most famous utility seeking, problem contingent strand within sustainability studies are those seeking to understand, and effectively manage, “tragedies of the commons” that, if not well managed, produce avoidable resource collapse. Here, scholars seek to understand key features of the problem – such as Ostrom’s fundamental point that some commons tragedies – such as fish that swim - cannot be served by “excluding” access – and hence require different solutions to effectively manage them. The point for the “problem contingent” classification here is that scholars like Ostrom and her students who focus on “non-excludable” tragedies, only develop theories and approaches based on these key features. That is, they do not research, and have little to say, problems that do not share these key features. There are many likeminded strands about developing problem contingent approaches by those who do not share the Common’s utility orientation, but do share is focus on how to effectively manage specified “on the ground” problems – i.e. they eschew considerations about “whether” a specified problem can be solved, to deliberating over how it can solved or effectively managed. For example, Tribe’s work on species extinctions rejected analytical universal approaches offered by extant “policy sciences” framework, to show how any consideration to saving a particular species must begin, and end, based on the knowledge about what kind of conservation efforts is required to maintain the viability of that species. While Tribe and others like him turned to debates in philosophy and the study of “ethics”, he rejected as problematic any effort to address species extinctions that might produce an outcome that caused species extinctions. Hence universal adjudicating frames about particular protocols for assessing policy dilemmas – based on universal theories of “ethics”, was in fact, highly unethical. Others in this line of thinking within international relations have pointed to the “on the ground” problem of slavery. For this group, like species extinctions, the only way to eradicate slavery to develop policies and interventions based on achieving no-slavery. The idea of applying cost-benefitanalysis, or alternative dispute resolution, to assessing not only how, but whether, slavery can be eradicated is anathema to this strands of the prioritization school because it opens the door to consider policy options in which slavery might continue. Hence for these scholars, adjudicating frameworks are always derived from knowledge to manage a specified problems (or class of problems with similar structural features and attributes, such as Ostrom’s non-excludability tragedies). These distinctions have led Cashore to elaborate four categories to include these broader distinctions (Table 2) in which we can now further narrow “problem solving” to “problem contingent” which more accurately labels theories and frameworks and course of action that are never meant to be universal, but only applicable to a class of problems Section 8: ## e) Examples of the Resulting Four Problem Types (4PT) Now that we have covered how the 4PT framework defines utility and problem solving distinctions, and reviewed examples of universal versus problem contingent approaches, we are now in a position to identify examples of approaches classified within each: Type 1 (utility, problem contingent); Type 2 (utility, not problem contingent); Type 3 (not utility, not problem contingent); Type 4 (not utility, problem contingent). This section will, out of necessity, repeat some of the examples above. This is by design: repetition of examples will provide clarity when the same ones are used to distinguish different parts of the classification framework. In addition, this part is important since the coding is focused on the resulting four distinctions – so understanding how the above review figures for making decision about where to place a range of literatures is greatly facilitated by giving these concrete examples. This review, in turn, paves the way for a broader discussion of four key schools of sustainability that reinforce, but are also much broader, than the 4PTs. While broader, inclusion of discussion of the schools is expected to generate knowledge that will facilitate and enhance and generate a nuanced approach to coding the 4PTs. Section 9: ### i. Type 1: Utility Dominates, Problem Contingent **Type 1** then sorts those that are motivated by utility chasing orientations, but narrow their focus to a specified “on the ground” problem that is seen as undermining utility enhancement. Those scholars that are sorted into this category seek to generate analyses and ideas for intervening that improve utility outcomes for that problem. These approaches target the problem or class of problems they seek to ameliorate. Their interest is not in developing universal theories to address any and all sustainability problems. This means that they generate recommendations and analyses based on their problem or class of problems in isolation from other problems that might have different features. And they certainly would never apply a theoretical or analytical framework to discuss whether the problem should be solved. There are countless examples of this kind of orientation. For example, I have a colleague who studies the issue of taxis idling looking for riders in downtown Singapore when it appears that they would make more income (utility) by driving to the airport. This problem then can be solved by reducing “information asymmetry” though some kind of intervention – such as advertising or sharing this knowledge, or through some kind of regulation reducing an ability to idle. In this case, interventions are adjudicated only for their ability to manage that particular problem - regardless as to the possible effects of the “effective policy intervention” in reducing overall aggregate utility – such as those case in which limited government resources are better placed on managing another problem that would lead to higher utility. As I discussed above and elaborate below Type 1 sorting would also include a large group of scholars and practitioners working on “collective action theory” in general, and its application to sustainability through the “tragedy of the commons metaphor” in particular. This is an important distinction. Collective Action theorists are motivate by eliminating utility undermining outcomes such as rubber necking on highways, hording of toilet paper during pandemics, or over harvesting that leads to collapse of fish stocks or water supplies. These approaches, which draw on the classic “prisoners dilemma” in which, in the absence of an ability to develop and comply with, a collective agreements, two prisoners in different cells both have a “self-interest” to confess because if they do not, but their partner in crime does, they will be handed a much higher and longer prison sentence. This “confessing/confessing” strategy is considered to the most effective utility enhancing approach given no collective agreement (an outcome known as the “Nash equilibrium) but it is not the most utility enhancing solution. The most utility enhancing solution is for both not to confess, as this will lead to lower prison sentences overall. In this case, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions to allow for both not to confess. In the sustainability world, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions such that harvesting of the resource in question is less than or equal to the biological rate or reproduction. In all these cases, analyses are focused on human satisfactions, but only around the specific problem in question. As I discussed above and in more detail below, Elinor Ostrom’s path to earning the Nobel prize in economics was sparked by her recognition that for some utility undermining tragedies, highlighted by fish, excludability was not possible. This mean that traditional interventions to solve this utility undermining commons tragedies that turned to private property or state ownership, and that assumed excludability was possible, were ill suited to addressing those utility undermining resource depletion tragedies. Ostrom then turned to assessing how to effectively manage these “common pool resources” (CPRs) which she posited were better addressed through local resource governing innovations. The point for coders here is that all of these efforts paid attention to the key features of the problem at hand – specifically those contextual and contingent factors that explained the particular utility undermining outcome for the problem at hand. Collective action theorists in general, and commons tragedy scholars in particular, only generalize based on the specific problem at hand, or a class of problems such as those commons tragedies in which excludability is not possible. They would never therefore apply their lessons to managing all problems nor bringing in an assessment as to whether the problem can be solved. They only look at the how to do so, not whether. Section 10: ### ii. Type 2: Utility Dominates, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) In contrast, **Type 2** literatures share an interest in understanding and championing utility seeking preferences, but they reject a focus that only includes attention to the specific problem at hand. The logic behind this, whether CBA that targets and aggregates individual preferences, or IR theories that target and aggregate state preferences, is that that group believes that it is unwise to assess policy options for ameliorating a specified problem without considering, and finding a way to incorporate, the result of this decision on other kinds of problems. Their logic is clear: if solving or effectively managing one specific kind of utility undermining activity – such as say, over harvesting timber – results in a set of policy and governance decisions that might preclude more lucrative activities – such as say the introduction of copper mining and its promise of increase wages – then policy officials have undermined their responsibilities in managing for the greatest amount of utility with which to benefit the welfare of citizens. This category also captures the IR scholars above who also seek to generalize about the processes through which states do, or should, maximizing their preferences – often in the name of the “national interest”- that are then applied to assess how – and sometimes even whether – the problem at hand can be effectively managed given state preferences. Hence those analysts that turn to state preferences as a way to think about how to manage and develop policy response – whether it be, as discussed below, Nordhaus’ reasoning that optimal state responses to the utility undermining impacts of the climate crisis will produce a 3.2 degree world – in contrast to the 1.5 outcome a problem contingent approach of the IPCC. It also applied, as discussed below, to neo-liberal institutionalist Keohane whose climate research turns to his theory of state preferences for policy solutions, rather than interrogating the contingent nature of the climate crisis itself. Hence whether CBA, or conflict resolution or a host of other likeminded approaches, Type 4 classifies those analytical approaches that are not offered as contingent on solving a particular problem but as a way to adjudicate the problem at hand by apply a universal way to think about, measure, and identify, and project policy interventions. Hence, these approach there expands consideration, and offering a framework for doing so, about whether the problem at hand **can, or should,** be effectively managed based on some kind of approach to preference formation. Section 11: ### iii. Type 3: Utility Does Not Dominate, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) There are many diverse approaches that are nonetheless united by their **Type 3 status** : i.e. analytical frames for understanding, explaining and prescribing policy actions that generate protocols for doing so that eschew utility motivations as a dominant force or outcome, and instead reflect on a diverse set of motivations with which to understand, and prescribe courses of action. These approaches are united by rejecting the treatment of non-utility outcomes as “irrational” and instead wonder how to understand and incorporate cultural values, social practices, environmental norms, that produce some kind of way to both understand the world and also provide the basis for recommendations about how to include a diverse set of ideas, organizations that generate legitimate process and wide accepted outcomes. As reviewed above when discussing universal approaches, while they reject utility as a dominant motivation, they share the idea that pre-existing theories and frameworks can be applied to adjudicate not only how, but whether, a particular problem can be solved. In other words, with these approaches – whether originating from a particular set of ethical frames in philosophy, sociology, geography or critical theory, one never knows for sure whether applying a particular one of these approaches to a particular problem will result in the problem at hand being solved or effectively managed. In other words, in contrast to the teleological orientation of Types 1 and 4, Type 3 approaches work from a preexisting framework – or develop a framework from a case that is meant to apply to any chase – rather than developing a framework that only pertains to that problem or class of problems. Section 12: ### iv. Type 4: Utility Does Not Dominate, Problem Contingent For these reasons **Type 4** frameworks are distinguished, like Type 1, on their ability to help understand, and ameliorate, a specified problem from the Paris Accord’s 1.5/2 degree objectives, to a particular species being threatened with extinction. However unlike Type 1 reinforcing approaches, Type 4 problems not only reject utility as the guiding force, they usually find that when those chasing utility outcomes are effective, they usually cause, or exacerbate, a range of Type 4 problems. The classic example comes to us from the identification of two distinct problems that are outside of the sustainability challenges to which Cashore devoted his empirical work. The first problem is that of slavery – i.e. the act of one human having property rights over another human being – i.e. “owning” them. A range of scholarly analyses assessing this problem have found that the only way to ensure slavery is dramatically reduced or eradicated is to begin, and end, with the required outcome: i.e. no one, for any reason, is allowed to own another person. Many apply relevant frameworks have not only found that (Type 1 and 2) utility chasing motivations was behind slavery in the first place, but that application of Type 3, 2 frames permits deliberations over whether slavery ought to be considered – which, by definition, undermines an ability to achieve the desired outcome. The second example is anti-colonial norms in which reducing or averting an attempt by one country to take over another can only be achieved by teleological approaches that beginning, and end, with the absence of colonialism. Scholarly analysis reinforcing Type 4 hence would reject neo-liberal and neo-realist IR theories as permitting an understanding of state preferences that could also consider colonial type behaviours, rather than assessing mechanisms for ensuring these possibilities are never considered. As I discuss below, a number of analytical frames within environmental studies are consistent with Type 4 reasoning. Section 13: ## f) Coding Challenges The above review will most certainly facilitate, and place in context, efforts to code qualitative documents against the 4PTs. This facilitates, rather than overcomes, several challenges that a code book must incorporate. First, while some documents may seem straightforward, others may provide a nuanced or even contradictory account that makes sorting more challenging. Introduction Dear reader, I would like us to get in a time machine for a moment and travel back to 1992. Let us land in Rio de Janeiro during the time of the United Nations Conference on Environment and Development (UNCED) or what would become known simply as the "Rio Earth Summit". It was a special time for a number of reasons. First, it was held 20 years after the "first wave of environmentalism" was punctuated by the United Nations Conference on the Human Environment (UNCHE), or what was known as the "Stockholm conference", which sought to coalesce and expand the 1960s and early 1970s proliferation of domestic environmental policies in the US and other developed countries through international initiatives. Second, Rio organizers recognized that global cooperation would require incorporating the diverse set of sustainability challenges faced by developing countries. They did so by generating a thoughtful and lengthy set of sustainability targets – from poverty to health care to education - known as "Agenda 21". Third, there was widespread recognition that the three interrelated triplet environmental crises of climate, biodiversity, and forests^3 were deemed to be so acute that halting their degradation would require immediate and meaningful international cooperation. This concern was informed by, and drew on, scientific research that showed alarming declines in all three. On the climate front a growing body scientific research (Charney et al. 1979) including a systematic assessment by the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) First Assessment Report (FAR)(Houghton, Jenkins, and Ephraums 1990), had wrung alarm bells. The report declared that, without a doubt, carbon dioxide, methane and other gases were creating a "greenhouse" effect that if left unchecked, was projected to dramatically and negatively alter the world's complex ecological systems. Inaction was projected to lead to loss of glaciers, degradation of coral reefs, changes in ocean currents, sea level rise, increases in forest fires and species extinctions. Scientific syntheses also projected exponential ecological impacts for every incremental increase in temperatures above pre-industrial levels. The first assessment's working group II (Tegart, Sheldon, and Griffiths 1990: ii) offered that their report would be "an essential reference for all who are concerned with climate change and its consequences" given that it provided the "scientific foundation" (Tegart, Sheldon, and Griffiths 1990: ii) for the third report (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change 1990)that which identified "appropriate strategies in response to the issue of climate change" (Tegart, Sheldon, and Griffiths 1990: ii). Similar alarm bells were run by scientists working on biodiversity loss, including Rachel Carson's (1962) famous calls to action, which was instrumental in catalysing the first wave of environmentalism. By the 1990s the body of scientific evidence documenting the impact of human's on the degradation of nature including alarming loss of biodiversity (IUCN 1980; Wilson 1992), but that continued activities would, by threatening widespread devastation of the structure and function of ecological systems, lead to an era of "mass species extinctions" (Wilson 1988). It was for these reasons that the Brundtland (1985) commission's called for dramatic protection of aquatic and land systems would be reinforced and repeated in a number of global synthesis reports about the perils of inaction (IUCN 1986, 1990; UNEP, IUCN, and WWF 1992). Likewise scientific research was documenting the rapid decline of forest ecosystems, including tropical forest loss, that threatened to overwhelm the lifeblood of the planet (Persson 1974). Several synthesis global assessments documented the decline of naturally functioning forests including intact old growth forests (WRI, UNEP, and UNDP 1988; FAO 1993). This comprehensive review of forest degradation (FAO and UNEP 1981a; WRI, UNEP, and UNDP 1986) recognized the interrelated components of the triplet crises, since forests structure and function was key to maintaining and promoting biodiversity conservation. For these reasons a number biodiversity assessments to include chapters on forests (e.g. Brundtland 1985: Chapter Six). Many of the scientific reviews of forest ecology included a review of startling tropical deforestation and degradation in tropics (FAO and UNEP 1981b, 1981a) (Poore et al. 1989) leading some reports to be developed to expressly to inform Rio deliberations (e.g.WRI, UNEP, and UNDP 1992) Fourth - and I know this is going to be hard to believe given the geo-political tensions in 2026 – it was a time when environmental concerns transcended traditional partisan and interest-based politics. Politicians from all stripes – including presidents and prime ministers – would personally signal their support in curbing the impact of humans on the world's fragile biosphere. In symbolic recognition of the need for all of us to transcend our individual and collective self-interests, the leader of the Rio summit was Canadian business leader Maurice Strong who had first made his impact in what was known as the "oil patch" of all things! It was also very personal moment for me. I had taken a leave from doctoral studies at the University of Toronto to work for the leader of Canada's social democratic party whose recent election was notable for electing Audrey McLaughlin as the first woman to head a major national political party. The pan Canadian approach to environmental problem solving dramatically affected how McLaughlin's team both pressed, but also cooperated with, the Mulroney government. This spirt resulted in the Canadian Conservative environment minister reaching across party lines to invite all of his provincial counterparts to Rio. This meant that my father, who was serving as the environment minister in the Canadian province of British Columbia – and whose government had just initiated policies to protect biodiversity, regulate forest practices, and begin treaty negotiations with First Nations – would join Canada's delegation. This bi-partisan orientation reflected similar environmental politics across the world. As for me, I would return to my PhD program one year later to research the role of environmental groups in policy processes, courtesy of "eco-research doctoral scholarship" that was launched by Brian Mulroney's *Conservative* government. My personal story was also not unique – it was played out in the experiences of tens of 1000s of individuals in countries around the world and within international deliberations who were united in common purpose. *The purpose of this book is to understand what went wrong.* How did the world move from, in the early 1990s, collaborative and meaningful efforts to develop and apply impactful environmental policy solutions, to today in which partisan conflicts, weak tools, and the influence of science deniers, have led to widespread frustration about the rapid decline of the world's climate, biodiversity, and forest ecosystems? *The purpose of this book is also to understand what to do about it.* My 35 years of research on these questions has made me recognize that the heavy lifting in making some kind of lasting contribution is to engage with other knowledge holders – including those who practice environmental policy solutions. It is only by moving out of our methodological comfort zones that we can, perhaps, find a way to change course. Section 5: #### a) The puzzle: acceleration of environmental policy solutions and problems The puzzle of the exponential increase in environmental policy innovations and climate, biodiversity and forests in particular, along-side their alarming environmental decline is both staggering but also, perhaps, less startling that say a pandemic, because the decline is measured in years and decades rather than seconds and hours. This time dimension – somewhat akin to the frog in the boiled pot – reinforces, rather than reduces, the severity of the environmental crises we now face. This trends makes my review largely an impossible task as it would take volumes to review all of the environmental policies that have been unleashed across multiple levels of governance in public and private spheres. What I can say is that environmental policy interventions first sparked during the first wave of environmentalism would exponentially increase since Rio (Figure I.0) to address an array of environmental problems from pollution (Shomuyiwa et al. 2023) to land use change. There have literally been since Rio, 1000s of efforts at global, transnational, regional, local and of course national level scales designed to curb environmental degradation of some kind (Mitchell et al. 2020; Mitchell 2003; Biermann et al. 2012; Young 2010). The UN would seek to catalyze much of these efforts through the use of "high level" meetings and international conferences (Figure I.1), as highlighted by its efforts to encourage countries to sign, at Rio, three conventions for each of the triplet environmental crises. As it would happen, negotiators were successful in achieving framework conventions for climate and biodiversity while failed efforts to do the same for forests led to the creation a year later of the Forest Stewardship Council (FSC) certification program which that introduced "non-state market driven" (NSMD) global governance turned to global supply chains for authority and legitimacy to advance, and seek compliance to, environmental standards (Cashore, Auld, and Newsom 2004c). This model was so innovative, and sparked so much support, that it would be adapted to address almost every critical environmental degradation challenge including climate, fisheries, agriculture practices and even commodity extraction. In the climate case these trends included generating specific policy reforms through hosting yearly climate "conference of the parties" (COPs), which would deliberate over, and witness, the coming into effect of the Kyoto Protocol, including binding targets on "Annex I" countries largely based in the north who had been responsible for the vast majority of carbon sitting in the atmosphere above pre-industrial levels. The Kyoto protocol contained mandatory reduction requirements of Annex I countries based on historical responsibilities. Subsequent climate COP conferences would catalyze agreements on an array of financial and market driven tools from "reduced emissions from deforestation and degradation" sparked at the Bali COP 13, to the "Clean Development Mechanism" (CDM) that allowed northern countries to meet their emissions targets by buying credits from projects in the global south. They also paved the way for the replacement of the Kyoto protocol with the non-binding Paris Accord that focused on "bottom up" local, subnational and national policies, as well as the private sector, as a way to achieve its objectives of limiting warming to no more than 1.5°C and 2 °C above pre-industrial levels. Disappointments over the effects of financing would lead to yearly efforts to find new ways to seek commitments including the commitments at Glasgow to raise $130 trillion for these efforts (Seymour, Wolosin, and Gray 2022). These efforts were matched by corporate commitments to achieve "net zero" climate emissions (Panwar 2021). The biodiversity case likewise showed similar trends. Indeed the label itself was then quite new, emerging out of the scientific findings on "diversity" of natural systems (Wilson 1988). While some argued that this had the effect of reducing attention on less heterogenous systems such as boreal ecosystems compared to the tropics, this attention helps place in context the Brundtland Commission's recommendation to protect 12 percent of the world's land and water from extraction. Across the world, there would be an increase in the development and monitoring of "protected areas" (Cook, Lemieux, and Kroner 2024) which, following the International Union of the Conservation of Nature (IUCN), became a key policy intervention to curb extractive practices. At this time the Convention on Biological Diversity (CBD) punctuated global attention and the UN would also use its meetings – albeit spread two years apart and given less international or media attention than climate – to reinforce existing interventions and create new commitments (IPBES 2024). Likewise, the Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species (CITES) which preceded the CBD, would expand to include more listed species.
