Certainly, Figueres (2020) and others would deliberate over lack of expected progressing, declaring to countries climate representatives in 2020 that the lessons from Paris – and presumably its missing ingredient - was the need to "set your minds to reaching net-zero emissions" which would then create the conditions to "forge the future we need" (Figueres 2020). As Hansen expected such declarations had little effect and by 2025's climate COP, projections were that not only would Paris' agreed 2 °C ceiling be overshot, but that the planet was soon to surpass 2.5 above pre-industrial levels, which would be expected to, exponentially increase ecological catastrophes including melting of glaciers, damage of coral reefs, altering weather patterns, and unleash habitat loss at unparalleled levels (Gayle 2025). Recognition of these trends would lead one environmental group officials, like their predecessors every years since Rio, to declare the missing ingredient was "a lack of urgency" (Gayle 2025). Meanwhile political scientists working within the Stockholm Resilience Centre would generalize from research findings to focus on implementation as a key challenge (e.g. Norström et al. 2014), that would reinforce their interpretations of, and what to do about, the latest Climate Cop meetings: "The Cop process *has delivered what it was designed for*: diplomacy and consensus" [italics added]"…but the world has moved into the implementation decade, and here the Cop is lagging badly" (Gayle 2025). In response to ongoing warming since Paris, Figueres, with colleagues, would revise her assessment, asserting that negotiations during the Brazil 2025 Cop were a "staggering betrayal" that is "no longer fit for purpose" (ibid). But what is clear from this review of climate, forests and biodiversity loss that if global efforts are to be measured by their ability to achieve outcomes, that not only were they never, as Hansen rightly projected, "fit for purpose". Instead, our focus needs to shift to unpacking an ability to contemplate these highly anticipated outcomes until too late – which goes to the heart of the paradox between environmental policy innovations and massive decline of the problems for which they were created. ^4 Figueres' shift in assessing the Paris Accord's impacts is also metaphorical for the countless similar after the fact recognitions that events did not unfold as hoped or expected. These processes in turn, have produced a second phenomenon: i.e. generating "lessons learned" exercise in the quest to produce "this time" effective results. For example, after 30 years of applying funding strategies of civil society, corporate sustainability, private governance and even scholars, the funding consortium known as the "Climate and Land Use Alliance" (CLUA) declared in 2025 that "this time" – in comparison with their countless efforts of the last 30 years – that they finally "got it right" about how to engage local Indigenous communities to advance climate, biodiversity and forest agendas (Johnson 2025). Often these efforts to improve policy design right reinforce their quest by calling for "evidence-based" research (Tombe 2018) and "data driven" answers (Maffei, Leoni, and Villari 2020) to better design, implement, "tip the scales" (Lenton et al. 2023; OECD 2022; Schmitz 2006), "scale up" (Hepburn and Stern 2008) and catalyze (Hale 2020) effective or influential transnational policy pathways (Bernstein and Cashore 2012). Yet the history of the last 35 y ears was that they coincided with, and hence appeared to reinforce, the environmental policy paradox. Yet as the future unfolded to reveal future evidence that differed from causal expectations, sustainability scholars and practitioners have responded by "doubling down" on, rather than eschewing, extant epistemologies (Sewerin, Cashore, and Howlett 2022; Howlett and Mukherjee 2018a) in their elusive quest to better design, and implement (Ivanova and Escobar-Pemberthy 2020) policy interventions. These patterns help place in context why 17 years after the World Bank's Zoellick (2007) incorrectly declared, that the 2007 Bali COP's turn to forest focused climate finance was a "transformative" moment that "*will reduce deforestation and forest degradation*", Mark Carney would proclaim, as leader of UN Glasgow public-private finance initiative (GFANZ), that as of 2022 there was finally enough climate finance committed to effectively managing the climate crisis (Carney 2021a). Yet just four short years later the coalition's business wing would fold.^5 It may also explain why policy designers have, in recent years, exclaimed that, unlike the myriad of post 2007 REDD+ initiatives, "this time" they have finally figured out how to create meaningful carbon credits (Greenfield 2023; Verra 2023b, 2023a), engage Indigenous communities to pursue an environmental agenda (Yeung 2023), and to accelerate decarbonization though technological innovations (McKibben 2025). The elephant in the room is that every year since Rio there have been countless such proclamations that either overexaggerate, and/or are inconsistent with future evidence. It also captures historical moments few remember, such as Al Gore's championing of the much-heralded Chicago Climate Exchange that was designed in 2002 to offer efficient ways to meeting emission reduction targets between US customers and carbon offset projects in Brazil that had, by 2008, collapsed. It also places in context Biden's much heralded as "transformative" climate legislation, much of which would soon be repealed by the Trump administration (Muir and Smyth 2025), and how backward looking research on British Columbia's carbon tax generated lessons about why it "worked" (Porter 2016) was inconsistent with, and not able to explain a future in which, just nine years later, in which BC government would, following Carney's first act as the Canadian Prime Minister abolishing the national climate tax and EV mandates, follow suit (Canadian Broadcasting Corporation 2025b). This elephant also helps put in perspective the decline in support and interest of the much heralded private sector "science based targets initiative" (SBTI)(Khan 2024) that had been designed to draw on technology and finance innovations (Clancy 2020a), supported and promoted by the likes of Al Gore and the Bezos Earth Fund, and more recently climate activist McKibben (2025)with which to help meet the Paris Accord's 1.5 & 2°C objectives. To be absolutely sure, the same Cop meeting produced proclamations, just as had every one since the first in in 1995, that a growing number of states and organizations finally recognized the urgency and escalating threats of inaction (Milman and Carrington 2025). As the director of the International Climate Politics Hub noted: "It's clear that a growing group won't leave Belém without an ambitious package deal built on four cornerstones: money for adaptation, better quality and accountability for climate finance, a clear focus on tackling the biggest sources of climate pollution – fossil fuels and deforestation…" and that the issue is no longer commitment bu whether "the Brazil presidency will choose to listen to them, with time running out (Harvey 2025). The same Cop meeting also produced, as had every preceding Cop, the results of extensive policy analysis into required actions to reduce emissions. In one example, the leader of Climate Analytics declared that their latest policy analysis gave hope to the world with its finding that if renewables, energy efficiency and methane commitments were met "it would be a gamechanger" that could avoid "climate breakdown" by limiting warming to 1.7 instead of the projected 2.6(Harvey and Watts 2025). My point is not that these statements and declarations were made with a straight face given almost verbatim statements have been made in every Cop over the last 20 years. Rather more startling is the strong causal belief that by making such statements they will cause "game changing" outcomes. All of this may place in context why, in 2025, Bill Gates stunned climate focused scholars and practitioners (Gabbatt 2025) by declaring that he found "a new way to look at the problem" (Gates 2025). After years of work and funding on these issues he claims to have discovered that emissions and warming were not the appropriate measures to address the climate crisis, that humanity was not in peril, and that the 2025 Cop Belém Brazil was "a chance to refocus" the climate crisis on improving human welfare, rather than being justified to act based on the environmental degradation humans have caused (Gabbatt 2025). He declared that, contrary to evidence of and projections of the effects of accumulating emissions (IPCC 2023, 2018) "climate change is serious, but we've made great progress" advancing the normative position the "it's time to put human welfare at the center of our climate strategies" (Gates 2025) as if this isn't precisely what had been occurring, as this book demonstrates, in global and local climate politics for the last three decades. He justified his pivot by saying the actual task was to help "people stay resilient in the face of climate change and that "I didn't pick that position because everybody agrees with it – it's I think intellectually the right answer" (Subin and Corba 2025). Section 6: #### b) Explanations I argue that existing theories relevant for unravelling this paradox are either important but insufficient, or misplaced. Section 7: ##### i. Orthodox: economic globalization, power and weak implementation **The economic globalization hypothesis** *H: efforts to attract global capital has placed downward pressure on environmental standards and regulations* The first overarching explanation is that economic globalization – i.e. individuals country's increasing reliance on trade, capital, and investment place pressures on governments to enact stringent environmental protections because doing so risks capital going elsewhere for higher returns (Bernstein and Cashore 2012, 2000). This argument turns to several reinforcing phenomenon that reproduced these effects. The first turns to post-World War II "Washington consensus" (Babb 2013) that liberalized trade will not only accelerate growth but that it will also produce, owing to mutual interdependence a "peace dividend" as countries, companies and international organizations recognize the importance of cooperation to maintain these benefits. Critics point out that while neo-liberalism certain has coincided with economic growth, it can cause, if left unchecked, social dislocation and environmental degradation (Speth 2003; Held 2004). The rational is not only the effects of economic activity but the incentives to relax regulations to address global capital – a phenomenon refers to as the "Delaware effect" (Vogel 1997). Ironically similar downward dynamics are also at play by policies advocated during Trump's second term to attract foreign capital through increasing protectionist policies – i.e. replacing the neo-liberalism growth model with another effort to accomplish the same outcome. To be sure, the economic globalization model performs well in explaining many cases of ratcheting down of environmental standards, such as (Dauvergne's (2001) historical analysis of the role of Japanese firms in shaping Indonesia's rapid deforestation during the Suharto regime. However, while economic globalization has certainly been used as a rationale for reducing environmental regulation in order to attract capital, but it, alone, cannot fully explain the paradox because we do see, in many cases, the adoption of effective environmental policies and regulations that occurred as economies were simultaneously growing and expanding their commerce – a phenomenon Vogel (1997)labels the "California effect" which as I show in Part III, almost always involves coalitions of businesses who, operating in jurisdictions with relatively strong environmental policies and regulations, align with environmental activists to increase environmental standards on their less regulated competitors – either through transnational (Cashore and Stone 2014) or international trade or environment agreements, or increasingly through non-state market driven (NSMD) innovations. Hence while important and complementary to my argument, this hypothesis is insufficient to fully explain the policy paradox. **The power hypothesis** *H: individuals, organizations, and classes, including businesses and economic actors actively engage to shape, and constrain, environmental policy and regulations consistent with their interests* Power is most certainly relevant as a great number of masterful accounts have shown that operate through overt (Lindblom 1977), covert (Bachrach and Baratz 1962) and latent (Lukes 1974; Herman and Chomsky 1988) ways to shape or minimize environmental regulations and policies at multiple levels (Gramsci 2020). There explanation is, to be sure, supported by political science theories on the role of global social structures and mechanisms of class reproduction in not only narrowing policy options, but also in shaping societal and government conception of According to a great number of scholars operating in this explanation the environmental policy paradox can be understood to be owing to the role of power organizations – especially but not limited to – business interests who seek to control and narrow the development, and deployment of environmental regulations that are either innocuous to them, or may improve their competitive advantage. This group would therefore look to the role of corporations and their associations in focusing climate, biodiversity and forests (Panwar et al. 2023) conversations to those that focus on creating a "business case" for action rather than problem solving per se (Busch et al. 2024). There, is, to be sure, much evidence supporting this hypothesis. After all, business interests and their association immediately moved after the creation of the FSC (Cashore, Auld, and Newsom 2004c) to promote business friendly competitors (Cashore, Auld, and Newsom 2004b)that have fewer and weaker standards, and or championed Type 1 efforts that were expected to reinforce firm profits over Type 4 constraints (Judge-Lord, McDermott, and Cashore 2020). Likewise many have found post 2007 Bali Cop efforts to generate financing for forests as a way to produce carbon credits entice profit maximizing firms at the expense of on the ground outcomes (Nerlich and Koteyko 2010). Likewise Levy and Newell have demonstrated on the role of firm in shaping international environmental agreements in general (Levy and Newell 2005b; Clapp 2005, 2004) while there have been a number of sector specific projects including Humphreys' (2006) account of global forest politics and Gale's (1998) research on the International Tropical Timber Agreement (ITTA), both of which found corporate interest working to marginalize environmental groups over extractive interests in advancing and shaping trade based sustainability efforts. Similar accounts have been applied to assess climate deliberations (Levy and Newell 2005a) and agricultural policies (Clapp 2025) and fisheries (Ponte 2008)Vandergeest. Likewise a great deal of research has found, or posited, that powerful organizations from the World Bank (Marschinski and Behrle 2009) to Philanthropic foundations (Bartley 2007a)deploy their resources in ways that emphasize market incentives over, rather than in service of, environmental impacts. These accounts certainly have strong explanatory power: the idea that powerful interests would work to steer the myriads of global environmental efforts to those that are either relatively benign to capital, and/or emphasize market and financial incentives as a prerequisite to achieve environmental outcomes, certainly helps account for the puzzle. However, there are three reasons why this account is incomplete. First, as I will show in the second half of this book, there are many cases in which business did not dominate or control the policy decisions. Hence economic globalization explanations cannot help explaining variation across time or across cases. Something else is going on. Second, and related, there have been, as my historical account in the next chapter reveals, a great number of business interests who have championed policies that went beyond their short-term concerns – from those who helped created the UN Global Compact to the Forest Stewardship Council that included the objectives to halt deforestation. Let us not forget that US Republican politicians in the late 1960s and early 1970s supported the Endangered Species Act, the Clean Air Act, and even overrode Nixon's veto to support the Clean Water Act. And as reviewed above, in the early 1990s politicians from conservative parties around the world who relied on business support were calling for meaningful environmental reforms. In fact, I have witnessed time and time again business leaders – such as then President of the World Business Council for Sustainable Development (WBCS) Bjorn Stigson give impassioned reflections at the 2005 TERI New Dehli summit on climate and again at Yale's Environment school following 2009-10 efforts by the Obama administration to advance modest climate policy reforms about the need for swift action. He left little doubt that he, and other business leaders, cared deeply about climate as an environmental crisis. In other words he represented and voice meaningful rhetorical concerns among sustainability policy and governance practitioners that cannot be reduced to some kind of Machiavellian plot to feign interest as a way to avoid meaningful action – even if that was the ultimate result. Third, and related – as I will show in Chapter Seven, sometimes, depending on policy designs, forward looking firms do participate in, and think about, how to ratcheting up of environmental policies qua environmental policies by seeking to generate effective institutional policy and governance solutions for doing so (Panwar 2025). To be sure, they still seek to create the conditions for profit maximizing behaviour and hence health skepticism must always be present, but it does appear that some firms engage without seeming to undermine or sidestep environmental problems. Simply dismissing these efforts would make it difficult to identify and generate the necessary problem-oriented communities, as I discuss in Part II, for identifying and deploying the conditions through which economic globalization might ratchet up rather than down (Barnett et al. 2021). Hence, by itself the economic globalization hypothesis is important, but incomplete. **The policy design/implementation hypothesis** *H: weak policy design and enforcement capacities undermines an ability to implement desired environmental policy objectives* A second group largely made up of scholars of public policy, public administration and business administration turn to research on weak policy design (Peters et al. 2018; Gunningham, Grabosky, and Sinclair 1998) and implementation capacities (Goyal, Pattyn, and Demircioglu 2025; Sabatier 1986; Mazmanian and Sabatier 1983). According to this group, previous policies that were not effective were the result, at least in part, of poor policy design/and or implementation (Fransen et al. 2023; Heyes 2000; Kill 2019; Kanowski, McDermott, and Cashore 2011; Ivanova and Escobar-Pemberthy 2020; Wang and Wiser 2002; Le Prestre 2017; Armstrong and Gunton 1995; Secretariat of the Convention on Biological Diversity 2003; Gavrilut et al. 2015; Ackerknecht et al. 2005; UNFF 2002; Norström et al. 2014). Hence, the solution that this group of scholars and practitioners take is to either redesign ineffective policies and/or create new interventions based on "lessons learned" from previous experiments (Nair and Howlett 2015; Hoffmann 2011; Sabel and Zeitlin 2008; Overdevest and Zeitlin 2016). Certainly this group of scholars is very important for generating insights about how governments and private actors might think of capacities (Sabatier 1986; Mazmanian and Sabatier 1983) and approaches with to help governments achieve their policy objectives (Demircioglu and Audretsch 2024). However I argue that the weak policy design capacity/weak implementation hypothesis is misplaced. In fact, believe that this explained the paradox has led to 35 years of concerted efforts to improve policy and governance capacity and financing for implementation, which has only served to produce an exponential number of policy innovations that are, themselves, unable to ameliorate the environmental problems for which they were created. In other words, those who hold the causal hypothesis that better policy design and implementation will "this time" make a difference actually o account for the proliferation of policy innovation and fragmentation at multiple levels hence reinforcing, rather resolving on their own, the policy paradox (Chan et al. 2025). We need to look no further than how this community's call to promote policy coherence over the last 15 years worked to reinforce the fragmented systems of global environmental policy innovations their work helped reinforce, rather than addressing the pathology that led to the proliferations in the first place (Nilsson and Weitz 2019a; Elmore et al. 2014; Lissack and Letiche 2010). Section 8: ##### i. Unorthodox: ill fit for purpose problem conceptions The argument in this book demonstrates that the first two hypotheses are useful but insufficient, while the third is misplaced – serving to, ironically, unconsciously and effectively reinforce Type 3, 2 and 1 conceptions while drifting away from Type 4 policy design capacities. A more complete understanding of environmental policy paradox is that policy analysts and advocates have, often unconsciously, generated "ill fit for purpose" problem conceptions that have made it impossible to conceive of, let alone deploy, interventions for effective environmental outcomes. Failure to recognize this bias means that like Charlie Brown and Lucy and the football, sustainability policy and governance scholars and practitioners keep trying the same thing with the same results. Only this game has lasted 35 years and counting. **The argument: four types of problems, not one** I argue that to understand why this paradox persists is to recognize that there are not one, but four kinds of sustainability policy challenges (Cashore and Bernstein 2022; Cashore et al. 2024; Cashore et al. 2019b). These are distinguished on two dimensions: does the analysis produce conclusions and theories that: 1) treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind "utility enhancing" outcome?; 2) are generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified and measurable "on the ground" problem or class of problems? (Cashore et al. 2019b; Cashore and Bernstein 2022). > Table I.0: Four Problem Types | Type | Problem solving dominates? | Utility dominates? | | :--- | :---: | :---: | | **TYPE 1** | **YES** | **YES** | | **TYPE 4** | **YES** | **NO** | | **TYPE 2** | **NO** | **YES** | | **TYPE 3** | **NO** | **NO** | These distinctions, in turn, leads to the conceptual and empirical discovery of four schools of sustainability policy thought and practice reinforce one of the four resulting problem types (Table 1). The Commons school (Type 1 reinforcing) largely views the environment degradation as a collective action problem that undermines long-term resource use (Ostrom 1990, 2003). The Optimization school (Type 2 reinforcing) views the environment through the lens of some kind pre-existing preferences – the most dominant and recognized strand is practice by self "environmental economists" - who incorporate, and convert, current and future environmental "externalities" (Stern 2006; Ocko et al. 2017) into utility enhancing equivalents (Thomas and Chindarkar 2019) as a means to champion aggregate utility seeking cost-benefit analysis. Another strand includes neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist theorists within International Relations who are motivated to uncover, and offer solutions consistent with, state preferences (Keohane 1984). The Compromise school (Type 3 reinforcing) emphasizes deliberative governance that integrate a variety of interests, values and organizations as a way to achieve legitimate and trustworthy outcomes (Bernstein 2001). The Prioritization school (Type 4 reinforcing) seeks to generate policy solutions that are granted higher lexical status than other problems (Tribe 1972). Each has sophisticated methods and prescriptive projects whose origins can be traced to highly ethical obligations about how to view problems facing the world, how to measure them, and how to identify effective solutions. The Commons school emerged to avert commons tragedies that threatened local communities with collapse of their resource dependent livelihoods. The Optimization school emerged as a way to enhance aggregate utility outcomes in order to create income streams to alleviate poverty, access health care, maintain food security and, when it came to state cooperation, produce interconnected institutions and markets that would realize each country's long-term interest in generating peace and prosperity. The Compromise school emerged as a way to balance competing interests in a manner that would produce legitimate procedures that would reduce conflict and promote consensus-oriented governance. The Prioritization school emerged to address and reduce the whack-a-mole excesses of utility outcomes such as treating the eradication of slavery, colonialism, and more recently Covid-19, as outcomes that must be achieve "no matter what" – irrespective as to whether they would or would not undermine consensus or utility considerations. *Birds and moles:* while their methods are often offered as a value neutral "evidence based" and "data driven" way to produce knowledge to inform policy decisions, they actually reinforced hidden an "unconscious bias" that biased one problem type over the other. All four schools produce unintended side effects on a range of other policy challenges in both synergistic or negative ways: i.e. they either "kill two birds with one stone" or they create "whack-a-mole effect" that create additional policy headaches. (For these reasons for shorthand in this book I identify the former as "birds" and the latter as "moles"). *Synergies bias:* the Compromise and Optimization – and to a lesser degree the Commons school – suffer from a "synergies bias". That is, they are much more tuned to identifying, managing, and even asserting the prevalence of birds, making them relatively blind to seeing the far greater number of moles. In contrast the Prioritization school recognizes and emphasizes moles as part of its DNA: i.e. it fosters an analytical technique that begins, and ends, with lexical ordering. This means that its adherents develop solutions for higher ranked problems that cannot be undermined by efforts to address second order moles subsequently. To be sure, while there are sometimes cases in which policies can be designed to create Type 3, 2 and 1 synergies, the dominance of the Compromise, Optimization and Commons schools means we are now in strange world in which achieving Type 4 outcomes is dependent on finding them to be synergistic with Type 3, 2 and 1 outcomes. *The overexaggeration of synergies, and minimal attention to, or disbelief in widespread moles, explains then the tragic paradox of Type 3, 2 and 1 policy innovations being developed to manage Type 4 problems.* While all are ethically motivated, the Prioritization school is the only one "fit for purpose" to manage climate, biodiversity and forest crises because the others either consider Type 1 utility enhancing resource extraction that almost always create a Type 4 mole of some kind, while Type 2 and 3 considerations of not only how, but whether, the Type 4 problem can be effectively managed. In other words, the Commons, Optimizations and Compromise schools produced Type 3, 2 and 1 policy interventions that are "ill fit for purpose" to address Type 4 "environmental climate, biodiversity and forest crises. In other words, debates about methods – reinforced by "evidence-based" and "data-driven" metaphors - have become so prominent, and each school defined by them, that we have largely lost sight of the competing ethical obligations that produced the methodological biases in the first place. I further argue that over time, the Prioritization school has become so marginalized vis-à-vis the Commons, Optimization and Compromise schools that most sustainability scholarly and practitioner policy networks have lost the capacity to think about, yet alone develop policy interventions consistent with, Type 4 problems. These patterns I show, reproduced over several iterations an "policy creation euphoria, implementation depression" cycle in global climate, biodiversity and forest governance that continues to this day. I further argue that the masquerading of underlying ethical motivations as objective methods has occurred so slowly and subtly over time, and diffused across scholarship, practice and policy design, that it has largely gone unnoticed let alone systematically explored - like the proverbial frog in the boiling water. This trend captures the shift from the 1970s when the Type 4 reinforcing Prioritization school was dominant in strongly shaping the content of the US Endangered Species Act, Clean Water Act, Clean Air Act and National Forest Management Act (Speth 2004) and that helped shape the Creation of the United Nations Environment Program (UNEP) (Ivanova 2021b) to today when Commons, Optimization and Compromise Schools are most dominant within local, domestic and global networks and organizations. I argue that this powerful but subtle shift has been facilitated by elite approaches to elite professional environmental management schools in general, and those in the US who tend to produce the majority of global elite sustainability practitioners, have been influence by, and reinforce the drift away from the Prioritization school towards Commons, Optimization and Compromise orientations. This shift, in turn, leads to a cadre of Type 3, 2 and 1 trained experts who, then hired in domestic, transnational and global bodies, assess the world, and conduct policy analysis and practice consistent with their methodological training, including how to think about what it means to be effective. As a result, and in contrast to 1972, those who develop Type 4 frames are often, again often unconsciously, marginalized or dismissed as either "radical" or "difficult". In other words, one reason this kind of argument has rarely been developed, let alone assessed, is that doing so carries enormous professional and personal risks given the hegemony of the compromise and optimization schools within professional training programs and these practitioner networks. The result has been a multifaceted drift away from Type 4 produce by multiple causal tentacles. Including reinforcing processes across epistemology, pedagogy, professional networks, technocratic norms, and governance practice. It is precisely because these influences interact — often subtly and unconsciously — that the paradox has both persisted and gone largely unnoticed for decades. In sum, I theorize a relationship in which sustainability policy and governance scholars drift away from Type 4 influences pedagogical and training decisions within professional environmental programs which then results in unconscious bias in graduates who are often hired in global and transnational environmental organizations (Figure I.8). These occur through many kinds of interrelated but distinct tentacles, such as reproduction of a scholar-practitioner "good sustainability governance norm complex" that teaches and researches synergies across all kinds of sustainability outcomes and formally treats Type 4 whack-a-mole effects, incorrectly, as Type 3, 2 and 1 design challenges. Likewise the vast majority of post program evaluations of success and ineffectiveness draw on techniques from the Compromise and Optimization schools, with almost no training in, or capacity, to engage in Type 4 program evaluation. *It is this failure to be explicit about these different approaches to, and effects of, their whack-a-mole outcomes, helps explain the disconnect between Type 3, 2 and 1 scholarly and practitioner efforts to address Type 4 environmental problems in general, and climate, biodiversity and forests in particular.* The relative influence of Commons, Optimization, and Compromise schools has meant the squeezing out of the Prioritization school, resulting in the decline of Type 4 policy design capacities. Without understanding and overcoming these biases the more we risk undermining the production of creative and analytical thinking necessary for imagining polices that have yet to be deployed and anticipating futures that have yet to occur (Morrison 2022; Capano and Pavan 2019; Peters et al. 2018). In other words, uncovering these differences is profoundly important if we are to promote "fit for purpose" analytical frameworks capable of identifying effective solutions for ameliorating the ecological effects of climate change, species extinctions, and, forest decline. This argument, in turn, paves the way for the third part of this book: how to bring Type 4 policy design capacities "back in" in order to nurture, and apply designs lessons that emerge, from Prioritization school policy analytics. Section 9: #### c) Assessing the argument If my multi-causal argument has merit – and the evidence in this book shows overwhelming evidence that it does – it also means that fully documenting and assessing the multiple causal tentacles would take a lifetime which is why, ironically, this phenomenon has gone largely missed. However, and for the same reasons, research can be undertaken to assess whether the argument not only has merit, but shows strong evidence, by focusing on critical indicator outcomes where, if my argument was correct, the empirical bias I assert would need to be present. This means that the first and most important step to assess whether the argument has validity is to conduct research on critical indicators that would need to and identify a host of range of critical indicators that would need to be consistent with the argument: i.e. that approaches to, and relevant changes of, sustainability outcomes, scholarship, and practice would need to reflect Type 3, 2 and 1 biases over Type 4 conceptions. This second and related step would then be to, as Part II undertakes, to reviews empirically evidence of the veracity of my argument. This effort not only provides overwhelming evidence consistent with my argument, but justifies Part III's focus on building Type 4 policy design capacities. Section 10: ##### i. Research design and methods If this argument is right, it carries profound implications for greatly expanding how to think about and measure explanations for the environmental policy paradox. It also confront longstanding debates about the merits of qualitative and quantitative research in the social sciences, solutions of which have arguably made it impossible for most to conceptualize, let alone empirically identify, the multiple empirical and causal tentacles that have led to largely unconscious "ill-fit for purpose" Type 3, 2 and 1 policy interventions for ameliorating Type 4 problems. Let me say that an ability to answer this question confronts almost every test book in the social sciences on research design and methods required which – though distinguished by quantitative and qualitative camps, are united in a mission to develop general principles and approaches for their preferred epistemologies and methodologies. Let me elaborate. One of the biggest fissures within social science in general, and political science in particular, is appropriate use of quantitative (Geddes 2003) and qualitative epistemologies and methods (Mahoney 2009; King, Keohane, and Verba 1994; Falleti 2006; Steinberg ; Collier 2011). There have been seismic shifts over the initiation, and response to, these struggles for methodological dominance. This robust efforts to emphasize quantitative methods and the search for universal understandings of the world within American Political Science Association in the 1980s as economists like Ostrom, moved to political science and promoted its "rational choice" strand; and in a similar vein, the behavioural revolution (Isaac 2015) that focused on public opinion studies (Inglehart 1977) greatly expanded quantitative studies that were meant to replace what they critiques as descriptive work in institutions and governance. However, these approaches have been criticized as not only expanding some sources of knowledge, but narrowing attention to others. This is important since the orientation does poorly at generate insights for changes in values that, history tells us, sit outside of their empirical focus, such as those changes in the first wave of environmentalism that were not derived from, nor understood by, behaviouralist research on preferences that experimentalism ontologies focus (Schultz, Khazian, and Zaleski 2008; Dolnicar, Knezevic Cvelbar, and Grün 2017; Terrier and Marfaing 2015; Kormos, Gifford, and Brown 2015; Gowdy 2008). Recognition of this also confronts the "experimentalist" turn and "nudging" rather also reinforce existing preferences to reflect on how to shift attention, rather than how to shift the underlings norms and values themselves (Gifford, Kormos, and McIntyre 2011; Griskevicius, Cialdini, and Goldstein 2008; Kreitmair and Bower-Bir 2021; Sturm et al. 2019; Zhang et al. 2018). Similarly, there were parallel concerns that these projects "black boxed" the state, and undermined the way in which "policies create politics" (Isaac 2015) led to a backlash (Shapiro and Green 1994) as the historical institutionalist projects promoted and practices by Hall (2003) and Skcopol (1995) who successfully ran a "Perestroika" campaign to be elected APSA president on a platform of bringing qualitative methods "back in". These efforts have also been shaped by anthropologists such as James Scott who practices "ground theory" in local contexts that generating incredibly importance insights about the world that others has completely missed (Scott 1998). I had the good fortune of debating, with Ostrom, research design during APSA's Boston meeting in 1997 during my Fulbright at Harvard. Similar debates were occurring within international relations, as the quest for universal insights through application of increasingly sophisticated quantitative methods led some to critical them for being so focused on generating universal insights, that they had, by default, to become increasingly narrow. In one major critique of this increasingly hegemonic group made qualitative constructivists, Bernstein and et al (Bernstein et al. 2000) argued that this growing community's focus on "precision" rather than "accuracy" led to rath benign conclusions, such as "neighbouring states are more likely to engage in war than non-neighbouring states" Recognition of these debates and counter debates is that despite their rigour, most of these efforts have produced a quest for guiding principles that do not base their recommendations or conclusion on their problem-solving potential but instead as a question to develop research design cannons that can guide their communities in common knowledge generating practices. While this approach has led to incredible knowledge generation communities, it has created moles by undermining required integration, as I show in section III, for generating Type 4 policy solutions. *An abductive middle ground* Instead, and for these reasons I apply what can best be called a "red thread" dot connecting narrative approach (Büthe 2002) that is aimed at generating and seeing a causal world that was missed by most qualitative and quantitative scholars, and that has had significant and important causal impacts in the very ways we unconsciously see, study, and offer solutions for managing the world's most pressing challenges. To this end, I place this book in the middle of two techniques that are "ill fit for purpose" for assessing the argument I poste: Popperian chasing universal understandings of the world, from those that give up by generating knowledge for a particular corner of the world. I refer to this middle approach as a way to generate "causal influence" logics that allow us to relax some degree of precision in favour of problem relevant accuracy. For these reasons, this approach is consistent with, and paves the way for, more in-depth "process tracing" (Collier 2011) efforts that can be applied to particular cases to assess drift. However, I also go further. I argue in the book that our quest in explaining the past has shackled an ability to think about how social sciences itself has, historically played a role in shaping the future, and, as a result, our ability to change the tragic pathway on which we are currently embarked. *Forward causality* The biggest challenge but largely interrogated by most of these cannons on social sciences research, is that if researchers can inform policy design thinking that might produce new historical critical junctures, the evidence that they are right shows up too late to inform the policy design. Let me repeat that. If the evidence a social scientist is right and might find a way to change course, the evidence does not yet exist because her recommendations, if acted upon, would create the new future. And she cannot get in a forward time machine to see how things went and then come back to the present to inform her design. While these challenges have been articulated by some for quite some time (Bernstein et al. 2000; Levin et al. 2012), mainstream sustainability policy and governance scholars and practitioners, tend to undermine forward thinking by drawing on elegant, but imprecise, quantitative and sophisticated modelling that as I show in Part III, these are shackled by Type 2 and 1 behavioural assumptions that cannot incorporate multiple step causal influences. The result is a strong emphasis in turning to backward-looking empirical research (Stechemesser et al. 2024; Pottinger et al. 2024) to tragically, seek to identify generalizable patterns of policy impacts and human responses with which to predict the effectiveness of policy tools into the future that, narrow, rather than seek to imagine new futures and that change rather than accept, extant feasibilities. Hence, this research positioning is also justified on the basis of overcoming the tragedy of "data-driven" and "evidence based" metaphors that undermine the creation of future evidence (Fukuyama 2024). In sum, both quantitative and qualitative methods are necessary but insufficient (Mertens 2015; Moon and Blackman 2014; O'Neill et al. 2013).The quantitative biased groups tends to go universal but shallow, while the qualitative groups tends to go deep but narrow. This means that I follow a "historical narrative" dot connecting approach with which to identify this phenomenon of the four types, and the unconscious bias, that had been missed by both approaches. Section 11: ##### ii. Structure of chapters The remainder of this book assesses the argument in the following steps. Chapter One takes care to review the 4PT framework by firs disentangle, the four problem types that apply to any problem, with the four schools of thought and practice that have emerged to reinforce each type within the world of environment and sustainability policy and governance. I detail several key dimensions to reveal not only their coherence, elegance and depth, but also how each would subtly work to reinforce and bias their preferred type. Part III presents the result of the empirical assessment. As a full assessment of the multiple tentacles and influences of these schools would take volumes and many lifetimes, I assess the framework against four measures that, if the argument has merit, would have to show evidence of this influence: sustainability outcomes (Chapter Two); scholarship (Chapter Three), and practice (Chapter Four). Together these provide overwhelming evidence in support of my descriptive and multiple tentacle explanation for the environmental policy paradox. Chapter One shows clear evidence of results consistent with highly effective Optimization school policy designs, important and positive results favouring the Compromise school's mission, and a mixed bag regarding the Common's schools concerns. I that also shows evidence that the rapid decline in Prioritization school problems were caused by whack-a-mole effects of effective policy designs promoted by one or more of the other schools. Chapter Three explores drift in sustainability research including unconscious "Achilles heels" biases in each school that have caused drift, and then turns to a select review of the way members of each school conduct research, ask questions, deploy meths, and makes recommendations for the future, that are all shape by pre-existing biases of the school to which they belong – including my own inadvertent culpability in causing drift through my collaborative work on conceptualizing "super wicked problems" (SWPs ). I then explore examples of these biases in sustainability scholarship governing climate, biodiversity and forests. Finally, I present the results of our coding on peer reviewed journals to show overwhelming bias in Type 3, 2 and 1 problem conceptions. Chapter Four turns to assess the role of drift, and of these schools, in the practice of sustainability policy and governance. I first assess professional environment schools train their students, including the result of our coding assessment on their teaching curricula. I then turn to review design and approach of "finance and market driven" (FMD) tools that, over 35 years, have drifted from incorporating Type 4 concerns to instead, advancing improvements in Type 3, 2 and 1 outcomes. I then turn to the practice of sustainable development policy goals to review a the role of a "synergies" bias that largely shutting the door on Type 4 conceptions as the recent reincarnation through the "Sustainable Development Goals" emphasizes implementation in synergies rather than deciding which moles to whack first. Part III then pivots from critique to hope, by asking how scholars and practitioners who unconsciously caused this drift can, and must, generate and integrative their diverse knowledge sources to reverse course. I do this by spending time in chapter Five the critical "integrative anticipatory policy design" skills that would need to be developed, and given as much attention, as we now give to (Type 3) dispute resolution techniques, (Type 2) cost-benefit analysis, and (Type 3) collective action problems. I then show, in Chapter Six the utility of this design framework in explaining policy success in failures in attempting to achieve the acceleration of climate friendly technologies, trade based environmental interventions, and the thorny case of jurisdiction wide carbon taxes. Chapter Seven then turns to the case of thermostatic policies and institutions to show their historical impact in solving specified policy objectives, and its enormous, untapped potential in managing Type 4 climate, biodiversity and forest outcomes. I conclude the book in Part IV by reflecting on how the books lessons might be applied in the practice of policy design so that we can "bring the environment back in". This task, in turn, requires incorporating theories and knowledge about how policy design learning might generate support from problem-oriented individuals and organizations who might be able to draw on, but not be shackled by, their own short term self interest in favour of building multiple step policy interventions capable of meeting, rather than averting, the Type 4 climate, biodiversity and forest's environmental challenges. I also justify my call for the development of an interdisciplinary "Marshall plan" aimed at integrating necessary research conducted by all four schools in which we may be united in common purpose to integrating what is now our individual efforts that can produce necessary but insufficient knowledge for managing Type 4 problems. Section 12: ### 1. The 4PT Framework The 4PT framework begins with two seemingly straightforward questions, the answer of which carries profoundly important implications for how analysts and practitioners see some problems over others, conduct empirical research, and make policy recommendations. The first question asks whether the analysis seeks to enhance *utility* of some kind. The second questions asks whether the analysis seeks to *solve* the problem at hand. These distinctions apply not only to the environment, climate and natural resources management but to any and all "on the ground" problems whether they are the focus of specialists working in health, social work, education, and even for that matter agricultural mining and energy. Notably, I use the term "on the ground" to refer to substantive, empirically measurable policy problems such as the release of a toxic chemical in streams, traffic fatalities, the possible extinction of a particular species, access to K-12 education, suicide, and so on. This term distinguishes policy objectives broader but more abstract goals such as "environmental conservation", "equity" or "human rights" which are critically important for framing conversations, but tell us nothing about what policy objective, exactly, is being targeted nor what policy objective needs to be achieved to effectively manage a particular problem {Cashore, 2007 #29279}(Cashore and Howlett 2004; Howlett and Cashore 2009). Put another way, although most analysts rarely ask these questions of themselves up front, they all, must, by definition, take approaches that answer these questions. It is impossible not to do so. My individual and collaborative efforts to think about these questions began in 1993 when I gave a presentation on policy learning to as side event at the Climate Cop in Warsaw Poland. They accelerated following a presentation to Copenhagen Business School's sustainability center when Professor Jeremy Moon suggested I expand my then three problem types to four. These efforts led me to distinguish each of the four constellation of the four types, identify and detail the four reinforcing schools, and, as a result, to draft, and redraft, with collaborators and research assistants and postdocs, a code book to facilitate coding qualitative against the four types. It is also important to recognize that the framework itself does not take a position on which of the four problem types is most important. Rather, its purpose is to render conscious what is often unconscious framing, which should be of assistance for anyone researching or analysing policy decisions and outcomes. It is expected that these 4PT distinctions will help uncover and improve work on policy styles, and even density and intensity of policies, that generally ignore the specific kind of problem to which the policy is being directed (Judge-Lord, McDermott, and Cashore 2020). The four problem types distinction also pave the way for the identification of relevant and more complex schools of thought that bias each type, the specific manifestation of which may be different across broad problem domains such as health, education, human rights, and so on. Just describing each school – including using the heuristic I develop below – will also help explain why they reinforce one problem type over the other. What the 4PT framework does and should not do, but paves the way for, is to develop a theory as to why some schools dominate over others, and what causes change over time. For the reasons the 4PT framework paves the way for assessing the argument I am making in this book about the way the Compromise, Optimization and Commons schools have caused drift away from Type 4 challenges. However, the framework can be used to understand and explain any outcome – including for example, those who want to understand better the impact and role of the Commons school vis-à-vis its seemingly similar, but actually quite different, Optimization cousin. This means that this chapter will also provide important foundations for those seeking to apply, and even continued to build, the companion 4PT code book. For these reasons the chapter reviews the 4PT framework in the following steps. First, I review "utility" and "problem solving" labels as the two key distinctions that position the 4PT framework. This is an important section given two distinctions represent the conceptual building blocks that are behind the two questions that pave the way for, and help identify the four schools. Second, I review examples of the 4PTs that emerge for each cell, focusing, for illustrative purposes, on non-environment cases. Third, I review the key schools of thought and practice that dominate the environment and sustainability policy conversations within and across multiple levels of governance. These combine to set the stage for a systematic assessment in Part III of evidence for Type 4 drift, as well as building Type 4 policy design capacities discussed in Part IV. Section 13: #### a) Conceptual building blocks: utility and problem solving At firs the task of distinguish whether scholarly and practitioner policy analyses emphasise, or not, "utility" and "problem solving" seems quite straight forward. *Utility* **The first distinction** was derived from my reading of the literature, and engagement with interdisciplinary colleagues both at Auburn University's School of Forestry, and Yale's Environment school, as well as interdisciplinary scholarly practitioner networks, where I noticed that many scholars and practitioners **believed** that enhancing "**utility**" outcomes were, and are, the key task facing sustainability policy analysis. It turns out that a large group feel that the more utility was enhanced, the better off society will be. Yet while this causal belief is widely diffused among a range of analysts, it is also hotly debated by scholars and analysts who either oppose utility as a guiding orientation, and/or seek other ways to frame desired outcomes. Regardless as to where we situate ourselves on this dimension, there is no doubt that this causal belief, and subsequent analytical orientin, usefully distinguishes distinguish sustainability policy and governance analyses. *The utility label covers a broad range* Cashore's 4PT framework notes that the term "utility" is generally applied and defined broadly. It includes for example, those trained in welfare economics who, largely focusing on income or economic growth, convert others outcomes to "utility equivalents" in order to monetize, and compare, human preferences for green space and nature, with their direct interest in generating income. The term "utility" also incorporates the broader philosophy of "utilitarianism" that seeks to advance outcomes that produce the "greatest good for the greatest number in the long run". It is also used more generally as a term to discuss whether, in terms of enhancing individual or collection satisfactions, there is any "utility" in a proposed course of action? For Cashore, the rationale for use of "utility" as a label was that it best captures the general belief behind a range of analyses that their goal is to improve "**human satisfactions**". This is an important clarification because the utility as a term has a decidedly anthropogenetic orientation in which policy is geared towards improving the interests of humans either today or in the future. This, in turn, is shaped by some kind of homage and widespread work developing theories relevant to unpacking human "**self-interest**". What is important for coding on the 4PT framework is that the concern itself is what is important – i.e. the utility label in the 4PT framework is shorthand to capture those who are motivated to advance human self-interested satisfactions that incorporate a range of more specific satisfaction seeking rationales. This orientation in turn, permit an exploration to identify efforts to promote and understand **self-interested behaviours at multiple levels** of sustainability policy and governance including individual, organizational, nation state decisions as well as those within international governance arenas and global governance agreements. Hence, the utility label includes, analyses focused on aggregating **individual utility**, **organizational utility** (such as companies returning shareholder value), or even **states' utility** by maximizing their own self-interested preferences. This category incorporates, rather than distinguishes, longstanding debates among neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars within international relations about whether self-interested, utility chasing states, are motivated to achieve relative or absolute gains. (The 4PT framework notes that debates about relative or absolute gains have undermine the similar quest to advance utility seeking interests, including, at the state level, relying on some conception of "national interest" that improves the satisfaction of its population through some kind of beneficial "rational" outcome). **Hence, this broad use of the utility label focuses on identifying any kind of analysis that is motivated by enhancing human satisfaction through self-interested preference maximization.** This captures in one category a range of scholarship and analysis that although diverse and nuanced, are united by their underlying orientation in emphasizing individual, organizational and state based preferences that pay homage to improving human "satisfactions" at multiple levels. *Problem solving* **The second distinction** came from my recognition that while some narrowed their conclusion to assessing how to solve or effectively manage the clearly specified problem at hand- such as such as saving a particular species from extinction, avoiding resource collapse of a specified fish stock, or limiting warming to 1.5 °C above pre-industrial levels – others brought in analytical frames that allowed for a deliberations over not only how, but *whether* the problem at hand could or should be solved. That is, this latter group applied adjudicating frameworks that were developed as a way to *address* any particular problem – regardless as to its unique features - that might find its way to the societal or policy agenda in mind. Rather than generating research and analysis to shed light on how to manage a specific policy challenge, this group was united by developing frameworks that allow for consideration of not acting at all, or acting in a way that does not effectively manage the problem at hand. *The problem solving label: distinguishing analyses that target "how", not "whether"* The term "problem solving" is also used broadly to capture a range of expertise and approaches that undertake research with which to solve or effectively manage a particular "on the ground" problem. That is, they eschew asking "whether" a problem can be effectively managed, and instead ask, given the problem at hand, how can it be effectively managed? This category then incorporates a broad group of scholars and analysts who are united in their quest to understand and effectively management an "on the ground" problem such as the extinction of a particular species or limiting warming to no more than 1.5/2 °C above pre-industrial levels promoted through the Paris Accord. This means that the analyses they bring, and conclusions they make, will go back to implications for generating knowledge useful for problem solving of a problem, or a class of problems. Similarly, these analysts will never apply universal pre-established theories to assess whether the problem at hand *ought* to be solved. This is not their interest. Likewise while their research may carry lessons for other problems, they do not seek to generate universal theories that can be applied to any kind of problem. Put another way, this group of scholars would, for these reasons, avoid any quest for developing a theoretical orientation, or set of recommendations, that went beyond the problem or class of problems from which their research was derived. The practical result is that many in this category tend to carefully assess problem structure or "key features of the problem" they are attempting to shed light on how to better manage or solve. This is important. The reason they eschew any effort to develop a universal way to address any problem is because, by disconnecting from problem structure, the approach applied elsewhere might not effectively manage or solve another class of problems – i.e. it might even make those worse off. This means that they are largely conducting research in which its lessons are "problem contingent". Any effort to generate lessons beyond a class of problems that is offered as a way to think about, and manage, all problems, is evidence that a scholarly analysis is not in this category. More specifically, any effort that concludes it is not "feasible" or "rational" to address a particular problem at hand that draws on a pre-established theory or framework that that was developed to analyse any and all problem is outside of this sorting category. Section 14: #### b) Four problem types The really interesting results is that answering these two questions leads to the identification of four ways to conceive of, measure, and treat, any range of problems that comes across the desk of a policy analyst for advocate for that matter. Type 1 includes frames in which a problem is treated as something that must be solved, and that doing so requires enhancing utility outcomes governing that problem only. Type 2 covers those frames that focus on enhancing aggregate utility outcomes when considering how to assess, and respond to, the problem at hand. This captures those approaches that develop a pre-conceived frame with which to compare, and consider the effects of solving a particular problem at hand on a range other problems. In these cases considerations are made about not only how and to what extent, but whether the problem at had solvable. Type 3 captures those approaches that also develop pre-determined frameworks that, in incorporating consideration of how solving the problem at hand might affect a range of to other problems, but unlike Type 2, champions alternative motivations for action to determine how to compare, and whether the problem is solvable. Type 4, like Type 1, develops frameworks in which a problem is treated as something that must be solved, but rejects utility or human satisfactions as the guiding motivation. Indeed most analyses develop to address a Type 4 problem generally view, or empirically find, that utility chasing policies were usually the cause of, rather than the solution for, the problem at hand. > Table 1.0: The Four Problem Types- Expanded Distinctions | Type | Problem solving?

pre-conceived analytical frame determines whether a problem is solvable | Before turning to identify the four schools that emerge from applying this typology to sustainability and environmental policy analytics, let us consider non-environment examples of each of these types. *Type 1: utility/problem solving required* One of the most common forms of Type 1 comes to us courtesy of those working what is known as "collective action problems". Collective Action theorists are motivate by eliminating utility undermining outcomes such as rubber necking on highways, hording of toilet paper during pandemics, or over harvesting of water supplies. These approaches draw on the classic "prisoners dilemma" metaphor in which, in the absence of an ability to develop and comply with, a collective agreements, two prisoners in different cells both have a "self-interest" to confess because if they do not, but their partner in crime does, they will be handed a much higher and longer prison sentence. This "confessing/confessing" strategy is considered to the most effective utility enhancing approach given no collective agreement (an outcome known as the "Nash equilibrium) but it is not the most utility enhancing solution. The most utility enhancing solution is for both not to confess, as this will lead to lower prison sentences overall. In this case, there analytical frame focuses on how to build and design collective action institutions to allow for both not to confess. These efforts have been promoted by "game theorists" who populate policy schools around the world. They often treat as objective their subjective framing of problems as collective action concerns that can be assessed, and treated, through utility enhancing rationalist frames *Type 2: utility/problem solving not required* The most common forms of Type 2 frames comes to the world courtesy of cost-benefit analysis (CBA) in which those trained within economics seeks to compare policy options available to address a given problem at hand not on the ability to manage the problem per se, but to generate a policy option that enhances overall utility outcomes – usually measured by income or economic productivity. Anyone working in any problem oriented applied school, from health to agriculture to social work to resources management, will be keenly aware of, and have many colleagues, whose methods and recommendations situate then in this type. CBA frameworks do does not just explore a range of policy interventions, but they also projects the effects of those policies on economy wide outcomes. If no option can be identified in which growth does not occur, then CBA assessments believe that the right policy answer is to do nothing. To be sure, CBA spends a great deal of time incorporating moles – what they call "trade-offs" by granting utility enhancing values to a range of environmental and social outcomes usually by conducing some kind of "willingness to pay" survey of individuals. However this approach reinforces Type 2 thinking, rather than considering non-utility enhancing motivations. However, CBA is not the only approach that fits in Type 2. For example both neo-realist and neo-liberal international relations scholars seek to generate theories of state preference, including longstanding debates about whether states maximize their preferences owing to absolute or relative gains(Keohane and Nye 1989, 1972; Snidal 1991).Their debates about underlying motivations that shape a given state's "self-interest" preferences mask their overall agreement that their theories and frameworks designed to explain **state behaviour in general** apply to any and all problems facing states. That is, they seek universal understandings of satisfaction utility chasing state preference to explain why states do, or do not cooperate, and the implications of this for not only how, but whether a specified problem can be managed. To be clear, they develop universal theories of state action that are not derived from, or limited to, any specific problem or issue states happen to be deliberating over.^6 That is, these theories of state action also inform recommendations from these scholar about how states might act go address a specific problem in order to better achieve their pre-existing self-interested preferences (i.e. how to "optimize" outcomes given existing understandings of state preferences). *Type 3: Not utility/problem solving not required* The most common forms of **Type 3** frames concern scholars and practitioners who seek some way to understand how, and to generate solutions for, individuals, organizations and states deliberate over a policy problems that incorporates multiple values and interests. There are many different kinds of theories that have been proposed over the years within political science and sociology to explain such deliberative engagement that is often aimed at improving legitimacy and trust within and across society and the state (Rosenbluth and Shapiro 2018) including Dahls' classic tome of pluralism (Dahl 1961b), corporatism (Coleman and Skogstad 1990) and collaborative governance mechanisms across multiple levels (Ansell and Gash 2008). It also applied approaches such as those who research, teach, and train in "alternative dispute resolution" (ADR) (Kelly and Alper 1995; Halbert and Lee 1990)and multistakeholder engagement aimed at getting to consensus (Coglianese 1996) rather than problem solving (Bryson, Crosby, and Stone 2015, 2006). Another stand of the compromise school has been nurtured by those studying philosophy which seeks to generate a set of norms and protocols for making difficult decisions – such as whether to save someone stopped on the train tracks that might risk the lives of many on the train. In these cases, pre-existing approaches for developing normative theory based protocols for doing so – not based on utility per se but on claims to higher level "ethics" are turned to as a way to have society and policy makers thinks about managing difficult questions – from species extinctions that results from enhancing economic growth – to saving lives during a pandemic when the isolationist policies for doing say may undermine children's cognitive development. In these cases what is important for the 4PT framework is that the adjudicating analysis that result are meant to help policy makers and society make decisions to address any problem that might come along, and are not derived, narrowed to, or contingent, on a particular class of problems. While diverse and unique, these approaches are united by their efforts to draws on theories of individual and organization behaviour as a way foster a compromise agreement among disparate interests. While drawing on theories that extend far beyond, and reject, utility motivations as far to narrow an approach to human behaviour, they share the same mission in developing a pre-conceived theory about motivations as a way to generate an understanding about not only how, but whether, the "on the ground" issue, or set of issues, can be resolved. Hence whether originating from a particular set of ethical frames in philosophy, sociology, geography or critical theory, one never knows for sure whether applying a particular one of these approaches to a particular problem will result in the problem at hand being solved or effectively managed. In other words, in contrast to the teleological orientation of Types 1 and 4, Type 3 approaches work from a preexisting framework – or develop a framework from a case that is meant to apply to any chase – rather than developing a framework that only pertains to that problem or class of problems. *Type 4: Not utility/problem solving required* Curiously Type 4 problem frames are rhetorically quite common among practitioners and scholarship, especially those trained, or housed, in applied schools including health, social work, education, agriculture, water, forestry, resources, and as I discuss below, environmental management. This is because the origins behind so many applied schools were owing to concerns that some kind of "on the ground" problem needed careful attention to helping address, and that, in most cases, unregulated Type 2 activities were seen as the culprit. This school's origins begin, and end with, an assessment of how a policy intervention might be deployed to effectively manage a specified problem – most of which were identified as the result of effective Type 2, 3 or even 1 policy interventions. Arguably the most illustrative example concerns efforts to think about, and achieve, anti-slavery objectives. A range of scholarly analyses assessing this problem have found that the only way to ensure slavery is dramatically reduced or eradicated is to begin, and end, with the required outcome: i.e. no one, for any reason, is allowed to own another person. Many apply relevant frameworks have not only found that (Type 1 and 2) utility chasing motivations was behind slavery in the first place, but that application of Type 3, 2 frames permits deliberations over whether slavery ought to be considered – which, by definition, undermines an ability to achieve the desired outcome. For this group the only way to eradicate slavery to develop policies and interventions based on achieving no-slavery. The idea of applying cost-benefit analysis, or alternative dispute resolution, to assessing not only how, but whether, slavery can be eradicated is anathema to this strands of the prioritization school because it opens the door to consider policy options in which slavery might continue. Another example is anti-colonial norms in which reducing or averting an attempt by one country to take over another can only be achieved by teleological approaches that beginning, and end, with the absence of colonialism. Hence scholarly analysis reinforcing Type 4 hence would reject Type 2 neo-liberal and neo-realist IR theories to assess how to address anti-slavery norms as its theory of state preferences sits outside of considerations required for solving the problem at hand – i.e. there cannot and must not be colonial activity even if doing so would more optimally realize state's utility chasing preferences. Hence, Type 4 frames reject commensurability biases of Type 3 and 2 orientations as permitting consideration of futures, based on pre-established theories of action, that may not resolve the problem, rather than finding ways to do so (Tribe 1972). They also reject Type 1 frames for narrowing consideration of problem solving to ones that, ironically, in chasing human satisfactions have been criticized for narrowing the human condition. Section 15: #### c) The Four Schools of Sustainability Thought and Practice Application of the 4PT framework to, with, and from, a systematic review of sustainability policy and governance scholarship lead to the identification of four distinct "schools of thought and practice" that reinforced each problem type: Commons (Type 1 reinforcing); Optimization (Type 2 reinforcing); Compromise (Type 3 reinforcing) and Prioritization (Type 4 reinforcing) To be sure there are a number of sub strands in each school that further work can carefully disentangle. However for the purpose of this book and other wide-ranging analyses, the types and schools distinctions provides arguably the greatest analytical traction for documenting empirical results in Part III and for generating design insights in Part IV. Likewise, there are, to be sure, other schools of thought and practice that reinforce each Type – whether exploring the environment or for that matter public health or education or social problems. Nonetheless, these four schools of thought and practice arguably represent the key forces of unconscious and conscious bias that have shaped, and changed, how we trains student and practitioners to view the world of environment and sustainability, bias empirical assessments and solve some problems that make other worse.
